query: promise all vs allSettled vs race vs any interview question

competitors.label: Promise API
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info has one of the clearest single-page references for Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any, including rejection behavior and ordering.
competitors.winReason: javascript.info is still the deeper API reference, but our page is now stronger for the interview-sized decision problem because it maps each combinator to a product behavior and adds explicit AggregateError handling plus the non-cancellation warning.
theirStrengths: It covers all four combinators in one place and explains how their settlement rules differ.
theirGaps: It is tutorial/reference shaped rather than compressed for interview delivery.

realWorldUseCases: Interviewers usually want one real use case per combinator: all for dependencies, allSettled for partial-failure UIs, race for timeout guards, and any for fallback mirrors.
followUpConfusion: these combinators do not cancel the other promises; they just decide what result to produce.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info frames the combinators well, but our page is now more explicit about the product behavior behind each choice.
actionableExamples: It provides more comprehensive API examples across all four combinators than our compact page.
followUpConfusion: Our page now closes the follow-up confusion more directly by pairing output-shape differences with an explicit non-cancellation warning.

actionableExamples: Add a small AggregateError example for Promise.any and a timeout-with-race snippet.

competitors.label: How is Promise.all() different from Promise.allSettled()?
competitors.selectionReason: This GreatFrontEnd page is directly relevant because all versus allSettled is the most common interview confusion inside the broader combinator question.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd covers the common all-vs-allSettled confusion well, but our page is broader and more interview-complete because it adds race, any, AggregateError, output-shape differences, and the shared non-cancellation warning.
theirStrengths: It directly explains the most commonly confused pair and does so in a concise interview-prep format.
theirGaps: It does not cover race or any, so it leaves the broader combinator landscape incomplete.

realWorldUseCases: all fails fast, allSettled always returns results, race resolves/rejects with the first settled promise, and any resolves with the first fulfilled promise
followUpConfusion: these combinators do not cancel the other promises; they just decide what result to produce.

realWorldUseCases: The page covers one frequent real-world choice, but our answer maps all four combinators to different coordination goals.
actionableExamples: Both pages include concise promise examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our answer more completely closes follow-up confusion because it adds race, any, AggregateError, and the non-cancellation warning.
